nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found from the surface to about 20 m depth in coral-rich lagoon reefs and shallow channels with high current; occasionally on seaward reefs

decorPreferences: live rock with numerous hiding places
areas of moderate to strong flow

diet.notes: An opportunistic omnivore. In the wild, has been documented feeding on microscopic algae, plankton, and small sessile invertebrates, and is known to prey on pest anemones such as Aiptasia and Majano. In captivity, generally considered to readily accept prepared foods including meaty items (mysid shrimp, chopped marine seafoods, frozen preparations) supplemented with marine algae and vegetable matter. Small frequent feedings are generally advised, especially during acclimation.

summary: An Indo-Pacific butterflyfish identified by two black saddle-like patches on a white body with fine vertical lines and a yellow posterior. Generally hardy compared to many Chaetodon species but typically considered not reef-safe; best suited to large fish-only or FOWLR systems with ample swimming space.
careNotes:
The Double Saddle Butterflyfish (Chaetodon ulietensis) is generally considered one of the hardier members of its genus and is commonly reported to make the transition to aquarium life more readily than many other Chaetodon. It tends to accept prepared and frozen foods well, which contributes to its reputation as an intermediate-level rather than advanced species.

Reef-safety is nuanced. Aquarium trade sources (LiveAquaria, BlueZoo Aquatics) consistently flag the species as not reef-safe, citing potential predation on mushroom corals, anemones, stony corals, and soft corals. However, Wikipedia and several reef-keeper accounts note that it rarely feeds on ornamental corals in practice and is actually useful for controlling pest anemones (Aiptasia, Majano). The safer husbandry stance is to assume it will sample corals and house it in fish-only or FOWLR setups, but some experienced reef-keepers have kept individuals with mixed success.

Like many butterflyfishes, this species is generally encountered singly or in pairs in the wild and is believed to form monogamous bonds; if keeping a pair, both individuals should be introduced simultaneously to a tank with plentiful swimming room. It is not territorial and grazes over a wide reef range, so a long footprint and open swimming space are more important than absolute volume.

Cross-source summary: Adult size is consistently reported at ~15 cm (LiveAquaria 6 in / 15.2 cm; FishBase 15.0 cm TL; ReefApp 15 cm; Wikipedia 15 cm), with 12 cm representing typical aquarium adult size. Temperature ranged from 22–29°C across sources (LiveAquaria 72–78°F / 22–26°C; FishBase preferred 25–29°C); averaged to 24–27°C. Tank minimum volume: LiveAquaria recommends 120 gal (~454 L) and ReefApp recommends 300 L; the larger figure is favored for an active mid-sized butterflyfish, rounded to 400 L. No reliable lifespan data was found across the sources consulted.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation, behavior (pairs, non-territorial), and a notable counterpoint on reef-safety: the species rarely feeds on ornamental corals and preys on pest anemones such as Aiptasia and Majano.
